title: The Invisible Hand of Gen-AI: Can AI-Enhanced Study Groups Improve Learning Outcomes?

study_design: We investigate the impact of Gen-AI and peer collaboration on student performance in an undergraduate macroeconomics course, employing a randomized controlled design across four sections.
In an undergraduate macroeconomics course at a large public university, we implemented a randomized controlled design across four sections.

The first section served as a control group, where students worked individually without access to Gen-AI. The remaining three sections were randomly assigned to one of three treatment conditions: (1) collaborative group work without AI, (2) individual work with Gen-AI assistance, and (3) group work with Gen-AI assistance.

Our experimental setup (described in section 3) allows a canonical difference-in-differences framework, a statistical method that compares the changes in outcomes between the treatment and control groups, to estimate the plausible causal effects of AI assistance and collaboration on learning outcomes, as measured by exam scores over time.

We conducted a randomized classroom experiment with 140 students across four sections of an introductory macroeconomics course, all taught by the same instructor with a common syllabus, materials, and assessments.

Institutional Review Board approval was obtained prior to the experiment. Before the experiment, students were briefed on the experiment and gave informed consent; ultimately, 130 of 140 students agreed to participate.

One of the four sections was randomly assigned as the control group. The remaining three sections were randomly assigned to the treatment conditions: (1) group work without AI, (2) individual work with Gen-AI, and (3) group work with Gen-AI.

All sections began the course under identical conditions: for the first three homework sets (HW 1–3), all students worked individually without AI. After completing HW 1–3, students took Exam 1 in class (baseline assessment at time t = 1).

Following the first exam, sections transitioned into their assigned conditions: control, group work without AI, individual work with AI, and group work with AI. During this phase, each group was instructed based on their treatment conditions to tackle the class assignments. After homework sets (HW 4-6), students took the second exam at t = 2, and after homework sets (HW 7-10), they completed the third exam at t = 3.

In total, students completed three exams and ten homework sets. Final group sizes ranged from 32 to 33, with minimal attrition. This design enabled a repeated-measures analysis of instructional effects.
task: Students worked individually or in groups, with or without AI support.
The AI tool, Macro Buddy, was built from course lectures and used a Socratic method to encourage critical thinking.
We tracked performance across three same-week exams and surveyed students on confidence and perceptions.
Although the course met in person, the collaborative group discussions for assignments took place outside of class (often via Zoom) at times the students chose for flexibility.
Group membership was assigned randomly (using the Canvas LMS feature).

To promote accountability and enable analysis of the interactions, students in all group conditions were asked to submit meeting recordings or transcripts of their discussions. Those in AI-assisted groups also shared their chat logs with Macro Buddy.

All three exams were administered in person during class, with no access to the internet or any AI tools. Exams were paper-based, proctored by the instructor, and assessed each student's individual understanding of the material.
The Gen-AI tool, Macro Buddy, was a custom GPT-based chatbot trained on the instructor's lecture transcripts, slides, and homework questions.

Macro Buddy was instructed to act as a Socratic tutor: instead of providing answers outright, it guided students by asking probing questions, prompting them to connect concepts, and encouraging them to articulate their reasoning.

To ensure effective use of the AI in a Socratic learning experience, students in the AI-assisted sections received guidance via course announcements (e.g. an F AQ titled "How to Talk to Your Macro Buddy"). This guidance encouraged using Macro Buddy to organize thoughts and practice questioning "why" and "how," rather than to obtain answers.
population: We conducted a randomized classroom experiment with 140 students across four sections of an introductory macroeconomics course, all taught by the same instructor with a common syllabus, materials, and assessments.
Before the experiment, students were briefed on the experiment and gave informed consent; ultimately, 130 of 140 students agreed to participate.
Final group sizes ranged from 32 to 33, with minimal attrition.
We gather baseline student characteristics, such as math comfort, prior economics coursework, Gen-AI experience, major, class standing, and residency, via a start-of-semester survey.

Baseline characteristics are generally balanced, although group discussions with Gen-AI section students report slightly higher non-freshman status (with 37% freshmen and 54% sophomores) and lower attendance.
arms: The first section served as a control group, where students worked individually without access to Gen-AI.

The remaining three sections were randomly assigned to one of three treatment conditions: (1) collaborative group work without AI, (2) individual work with Gen-AI assistance, and (3) group work with Gen-AI assistance.

One of the four sections was randomly assigned as the control group. The remaining three sections were randomly assigned to the treatment conditions: (1) group work without AI, (2) individual work with Gen-AI, and (3) group work with Gen-AI.
For clarity and consistency, we refer to these as Sections 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, in the consort flow diagram presented in Figure 1.
Section 1: Individual Work, no AI, n = 32 (excluded = 3)
Section 4: AI+GD (Group discussion with Generative AI), n = 33
outcome_definitions: Student performance was assessed across three exams.
The exam score is out of 20.
Some students could earn extra credit, leading to scores above 20.
We also collect perceptions of understanding and critical thinking after the second and third exams.
We measure attendance as the percentage of classes attended.
statistical_results: Students in all three treatment groups outperformed those in the control group.

Notably, the combination of Gen-AI and group work yielded the highest gains, suggesting that these two approaches are complementary: while Gen-AI promotes individual reasoning, group collaboration facilitates peer explanation and clarification.
Students in all three treatment groups outperform the control group on exam scores, with the group discussion with the Gen-AI section showing the highest mean (16.04 vs. 14.36 in the control).
The estimated intercept ˆα = 17.805 reflects the control group's baseline average score.

The coefficient of T reatmentis ˆβ = 0.828, which captures the baseline difference between the group discussion and control groups, or 18 .633− 17.805 ≈ 0.828, indicating minor group-level differences.
The coefficient of P ostor ˆγ =−6.320 reflects the control group's score drop from baseline to second exam or 11 .484− 17.805 ≈−6.320, suggesting the second exam is more difficult.

The coefficient of T reatment× P ostis ˆδ = 0.984 represents the difference-in-differences estimate: while group discussion students drop by 5.336 points 13 .297− 18.633 ≈−5.336, the control group drops by 6.320 as given by ˆ γ, yielding a treatment effect as −5.336−(−6.320) =−5.336+ 6.320 ≈ 0.984.
In column (2), comparing the third exam to the baseline, students in the group discussion section score 1.275 points higher on average than the control group.
In columns (3) and (4), students in the Gen-AI section outperform the control group by 2.237 and 2.385 points on the second and third exams, respectively.
In columns (5) and (6), students in the combined group discussion and Gen-AI section outperform the control group by 1.676 points on the second exam and 2.751 points on the third exam.

Table 2 reports estimates from the difference-in-differences model specified in Equation 1. Odd-numbered columns (1), (3), and (5) compare second exam scores for the sections assigned to group discussion, Gen-AI, and combined treatment groups, respectively, to the control group. Even-numbered columns (2), (4), and (6) present the same comparisons for the third exam.

Table 3 extends the previous difference-in-differences analysis by including controls that account for student-level heterogeneity in observable student characteristics, such as math comfort, prior economics coursework, macroeconomic confidence, AI experience, group preference, in-state status, business major, class standing, and attendance.

Across all six columns, the interaction term T reatment× P ostcaptures the causal effect of each treatment relative to the control group over time, remains positive and slightly higher than the respective estimates presented in Table 1, confirming that all three instructional strategies continue to improve student performance over time, even after adjusting for covariates.
Among the control variables, higher attendance consistently correlates with better exam performance.

Business majors perform significantly better on the third exam under the group discussion treatment, while math comfort is positively associated with outcomes, especially in the group discussion with Gen-AI condition.

All treatment groups experienced a less pronounced decline in scores on the second exam and showed stronger recovery by the third exam, with the combination of group discussion and Gen-AI yielding the most pronounced improvement.
ambiguity_or_caveats: While students in the control group were explicitly instructed not to use AI tools during the study period, we acknowledge that incidental use outside of structured sessions could not be fully ruled out.
Nonetheless, our results still reflect the effects of " structured", "guided" use of GPT-based chatbot ( Macro Buddy ), rather than mere access to Gen-AI tools.
Because exams were proctored and AI use was prohibited, we interpret the performance gains as resulting from the structured use of Macro Buddy during assignments, rather than during evaluation.

In all our analyses, we produce conservative standard errors that are robust to heteroskedasticity. However, due to the two-section comparison using difference-in-differences, we do not cluster the standard errors at the student's section level. Clustered at the section would result in standard errors being close to zero.
This was a single-course experiment, which constrains external validity.
Some observed benefits may reflect a novelty effect, and we did not track long-term retention.

Moreover, some control students may have accessed public AI tools, which could attenuate treatment effects. If contamination occurred, our results represent a conservative lower bound on true effects.

Finally, we emphasize the equity dimension. Without intentional design, Gen-AI may exacerbate existing disparities by disproportionately aiding already-advantaged students or reducing active engagement. It is essential to monitor how AI affects both opportunity and effort in learning.

notes: This is an original empirical study with a randomized controlled trial design. The paper tests the impact of AI-enhanced study groups (Macro Buddy, a custom GPT-based Socratic tutor) combined with peer collaboration on student learning outcomes in an introductory macroeconomics course. The study compares four conditions: control (individual work, no AI), group discussion without AI, individual work with AI, and group discussion with AI. Outcomes are measured via three exams administered over the semester, with difference-in-differences estimation used to identify causal effects. The paper also includes survey data on student confidence and perceptions.
